stem: A 15-year-old girl attends a metropolitan general practice for a sports clearance before joining a school netball squad. She arrives with her mother, who fills out the registration form and remains in the consulting room while the patient gives a brief history of an ankle sprain six weeks ago that has fully resolved. The patient is fully immunised, takes no regular medications, and her growth chart sits along the 50th centile for height and weight. The general practitioner has 20 minutes booked and intends to perform a HEEADSSS psychosocial screen as part of the routine adolescent encounter. The mother is keen to stay and mentions in passing that the patient has been 'a bit moody' but she is sure it is just school stress. The patient looks at the floor when this is said and gives short answers. There is no history of self-harm, no current school refusal, and the academic report card last term was unchanged. The practice has private rooms available and a chaperone is offered. Pre-consult observations are unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72 bpm, BP 112/68 mmHg, RR 14 /min, T 36.7 deg C, BMI 21.3 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Healed ankle, full range of movement, no joint laxity, mood guarded
data_table.Social context: Year 10 student, lives with mother and younger sister, parents separated 2 years ago
data_table.History: Fully immunised, no previous mental health contact

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate way to begin the HEEADSSS assessment?

options.text: Ask the mother to leave, then explain confidentiality and its mandatory reporting limits clearly before any HEEADSSS questions
options.rationale: The standard adolescent consultation structure is parent out, confidentiality and its mandatory-reporting limits explained, then HEEADSSS progressing from least to most sensitive domains, as per RACP GP Resource Kit and NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework.
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health
NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework

options.text: Proceed with the mother in the room and start with Home and Education questions as a way to build rapport gradually
options.rationale: Parental presence systematically suppresses disclosure of risk behaviour; HEEADSSS is conducted with the adolescent alone after explicit confidentiality framing. Would be reasonable only as warm-up small talk before the parent leaves (Source: RACP Adolescent Health GP Resource Kit).
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health

options.text: Open with the Sexuality and Suicide domains immediately while the rapport built from the joint history is still warm
options.rationale: HEEADSSS deliberately starts with low-threat domains (Home, Education) and saves Sexuality and Suicide for after rapport. Opening with high-threat domains is the disclosure-killing anti-pattern (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health

options.text: Defer the HEEADSSS assessment to a follow-up visit and only address the sports clearance ankle today since rapport is limited
options.rationale: Every adolescent encounter is a HEEADSSS opportunity; deferral risks missing the disclosure the guarded presentation suggests. Would be acceptable only if the patient refuses, with explicit return-to-it next visit (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health

options.text: Give the patient a paper HEEADSSS self report questionnaire to complete alone in the waiting room before the consultation
options.rationale: Self-administered screens can supplement but never replace structured face-to-face interview; subtle cues and follow-up questions are lost. Would be appropriate only as an adjunct to direct interview (Source: NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework).
source_refs: NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework

explanation.summary: HEEADSSS begins with separating the parent, framing confidentiality and its limits, and then progressing from low-threat to high-threat domains (Home, Education, Eating, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/Self-harm, Safety), as per the RACP GP Resource Kit.
key_points: Always interview adolescent alone for at least part of the consult
Frame confidentiality and its mandatory limits BEFORE questions
Order matters: lower threat domains first, sexuality and suicide later
Every encounter is a HEEADSSS opportunity
Self-report screens supplement, never replace, the interview
explanation.pearls: Maternal 'just school stress' framing in front of a guarded teenager is a hook to politely separate, not to defer.

sources.label: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health
NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to a youth health clinic alone after school for a contraception and STI check before a planned first sexual encounter with a same-age girlfriend. He is articulate, knows about condoms, denies coercion, and reports no drug use. The nurse practitioner introduces herself, offers a chaperone, and prepares to start a HEEADSSS screen. The patient interrupts to ask whether anything he says today will be told to his mother, his school, or the police. He specifically wants to know what would trigger the clinician to break confidentiality before he answers any further questions. He has no previous mental health contact, has not self-harmed, and there is no current disclosure of abuse. He has read on a youth website that 'doctors keep everything secret under 16' and wants to know if this is correct. He is fully immunised, in Year 9, lives with both biological parents, and there is no Child Protection history known to the practice.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14 /min, T 36.6 deg C
data_table.Examination: Well appearing, age-appropriate Tanner staging, no signs of injury
data_table.Social context: Year 9, lives with parents, no Child Protection involvement
data_table.History: No prior mental health contact, no self-harm history

lead_in: Which framing of confidentiality limits is the single most appropriate response?

options.text: Promise full unconditional confidentiality for today's entire consultation because he is plainly competent and the topic raised today is only contraception advice
options.rationale: Unconditional promises misrepresent the legal duty to disclose risk of serious harm and destroy trust when a later break becomes necessary. Would never be correct; framing the limits up-front protects the relationship (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health

options.text: Explain that conversation is private unless he discloses serious risk to himself, risk to others, or abuse, in which case agencies are informed
options.rationale: The standard adolescent confidentiality script names the three limits up front (self-harm risk, harm to others, abuse) before the interview begins, as per RACP GP Resource Kit and NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework.
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health
NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework

options.text: Explain that everything will be shared with both of his parents today because he is currently aged under 16 years
options.rationale: Parental disclosure is not automatic under 16 in Australia; the Gillick framework permits confidential care if the adolescent is competent. Would be correct only if the adolescent is not Gillick-competent and parental involvement is in their best interest (Source: RACGP; RACP).
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health

options.text: Tell him that only the disclosure of sexual activity will trigger a notification today and that drug use questions are entirely private under all circumstances
options.rationale: Confidentiality limits are tied to risk of serious harm and abuse, not to which topic is discussed; carving out drug use as private misrepresents the duty. Would never be correct (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
source_refs: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health

options.text: Defer the entire conversation today until one of his parents or a legal guardian can attend to consent to confidentiality
options.rationale: Confidential adolescent care without a parent present is the recommended model; parental consent is not required to interview a Gillick-competent young person. Would be appropriate only if the adolescent is clearly not Gillick-competent (Source: RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality Guidance).

explanation.summary: The standard adolescent confidentiality frame names three limits up front: risk of serious harm to self, risk of serious harm to others, and current abuse. This is delivered BEFORE the HEEADSSS interview so the young person can give informed consent to the conversation.
key_points: Confidentiality is conditional, not absolute
Limits = harm to self, harm to others, abuse
Limits are explained BEFORE questions, not after disclosure
Under 16 does not automatically mean parental disclosure
Drug, sex and mental health content alone do not breach confidentiality
explanation.pearls: Adolescents test the boundary first; a clear script buys trust for the rest of the consult.

sources.label: RACP GP Resource Kit Adolescent Health
NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework

stem: A 15-year-old girl attends a community general practice in NSW alone, requesting the etonogestrel subdermal implant for contraception. She has been in a relationship with a 16-year-old boyfriend for six months and they have used condoms consistently. She explains that she does not want to involve her mother because of cultural concerns but agrees that talking to a parent could be helpful in principle. She knows the implant works for three years, can give the side effect profile including irregular bleeding and possible weight change, and demonstrates that she has thought about what she would do if condom use lapses. She denies coercion, denies pressure to be sexually active, and there is no power imbalance in the relationship. She has no medical conditions that would alter the contraceptive choice and is not at risk of pregnancy today. Her HEEADSSS screen is otherwise reassuring, with no self-harm, no substance use beyond occasional alcohol, and good school engagement. There is no Child Protection history and the relationship is not subject to mandatory reporting.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 70 bpm, BP 108/64 mmHg, BMI 23 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Well, no contraindication to LARC identified
data_table.Social context: Year 10, no power imbalance, no coercion, consensual relationship
data_table.History: Regular cycles, no VTE history, non-smoker

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate course of action?

options.text: Decline to prescribe any hormonal contraception today because formal parental consent is required for every patient under the age of 16
options.rationale: AU law follows Gillick: a competent minor may consent to contraception without parental involvement. Blanket parental-consent rule is the legal anti-pattern. Would be appropriate only if Gillick competence is not established (Source: Family Planning NSW; RACGP).

options.text: Make a mandatory report to NSW Child Protection today on the basis that the patient is sexually active under the age of 16
options.rationale: NSW mandatory reporting is triggered by risk of significant harm, not consensual age-appropriate sex in a non-exploitative relationship. Would be correct if the partner were much older, coercion present, or other RoSH features apply (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).

options.text: Assess and document Gillick competence today, encourage but do not require parental involvement, and proceed with the implant
options.rationale: A documented Gillick competence assessment with encouragement of parental involvement is the standard Australian approach to under-16 contraception when the young person is competent and the relationship is not exploitative, as per Family Planning NSW and RACGP.

options.text: Prescribe condoms alone today and review her in 12 months once she has turned 16 to revisit longer acting reversible options
options.rationale: Withholding LARC from a competent minor who has requested it and is at ongoing pregnancy risk is not consistent with Gillick. Condoms-only delay would be appropriate only if she chose that method after counselling (Source: Family Planning NSW).

options.text: Insist that she bring her mother to the next appointment before any form of contraception can be prescribed for her
options.rationale: Parental involvement is encouraged but never a precondition once Gillick competence is documented. Would be correct only if competence not established and the adolescent agrees to involve a parent (Source: RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality Guidance).

explanation.summary: Australian law applies the Gillick competence test for contraception in under-16s: a competent minor may consent. The clinician documents the competence assessment, encourages parental involvement, and considers whether the relationship is exploitative before prescribing.
key_points: Gillick competence is decision-specific (this decision, today)
Encourage parental involvement, do not require it
Consensual same-age relationships are not automatically reportable in NSW
Document understanding of method, side effects, risks, alternatives
Screen for coercion and power imbalance
explanation.pearls: Encourage parental involvement; do not require it once Gillick competence is documented.

stem: A 15-year-old girl presents to a Victorian general practice with her 18-year-old sister. She has confirmed pregnancy at 7 weeks by dating ultrasound today and is requesting termination of pregnancy. The biological father is her 16-year-old boyfriend and the relationship is described by both as consensual and non-coercive. The patient does not want to tell either parent at present and asks specifically about confidentiality. She can describe both medical (mifepristone with misoprostol) and surgical termination options, explains the risks of each in age-appropriate language, and articulates that continuing the pregnancy is not what she wants. Her HEEADSSS screen reveals no self-harm, no substance use, and her mood is appropriately anxious but not depressed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peritonism, no PV bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 22,000 IU/L, dating scan 7+0 weeks intrauterine, blood group O Rh positive, Hb 128 g/L
data_table.Social context: Year 10, stable home, sister supporting, no coercion
data_table.History: G1P0, no medical conditions, no medications

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer the case to the Victorian Children's Court for authorisation because termination decisions for under 16s require judicial sanction
options.rationale: TOP under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 does not require court authorisation for competent minors; Gillick is the test. Would be correct only if no Australian jurisdiction had legislated the test (i.e., never) (Source: RANZCOG; Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Mandatorily report the pregnancy to Victorian Child Protection today because the patient is under 16 and currently pregnant
options.rationale: Pregnancy in an under-16 is not itself mandatorily reportable in Victoria; threshold is significant harm considering age difference, coercion and exploitation. Would be correct if the partner is significantly older or coercion present (Source: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting).
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

options.text: Require written parental consent from at least one parent before any termination referral because Gillick does not apply to termination decisions
options.rationale: Gillick applies to all medical decisions including TOP; parental consent is encouraged not required. Would be correct only with proven incompetence (Source: RANZCOG).

options.text: Document Gillick competence today, maintain confidentiality, encourage parental involvement, and refer for the patient's chosen termination pathway
options.rationale: A competent minor may consent to termination of pregnancy in Victoria; the clinician documents Gillick competence, encourages but does not require parental involvement, and refers for the patient's chosen pathway, as per RANZCOG and the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008.

options.text: Defer the decision by two weeks of additional counselling because the patient is too young to consent in the first trimester
options.rationale: Mandated cooling-off is not part of Victorian termination law and delay risks pushing past medical-TOP window (under 9 weeks). Would never be correct as a statutory step (Source: RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: In Victoria, a Gillick competent minor may consent to termination of pregnancy without parental consent or court authorisation. The clinician documents the competence assessment, encourages parental involvement where safe and acceptable, and refers within the Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 framework.
key_points: Gillick competence applies to termination decisions
Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 allows medical termination to 24 weeks
Parental involvement encouraged, not required
Pregnancy in under-16 is not automatically a Child Protection notification
Delay risks medical termination eligibility (typically <=9 weeks)
explanation.pearls: Court authorisation is reserved for non-therapeutic interventions; therapeutic terminations rely on Gillick.

sources.label: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

stem: A general practitioner working in a community practice in NSW reviews a 6-year-old boy brought in by his maternal grandmother for assessment of recurrent settled cough. The history reveals that the child has been living with the grandmother for two months because the mother is in a methamphetamine-related relapse and the maternal home is unsafe. The boy is fully immunised, has met all developmental milestones, and his growth chart sits at the 25th centile. The grandmother reports that the mother has been violent toward the child's stepfather during intoxication episodes, but the child has not directly witnessed this in the past month while staying with her. The grandmother has informal care, has been in contact with the maternal aunt and a school welfare officer, and is aware the mother is engaged with an alcohol and other drugs service. The child shows no signs of physical injury, no behavioural regression, and his school attendance has improved since moving in with the grandmother. No prior Child Protection report has been made. The cough is from a settled URTI and not the concern of the consultation. The GP is considering whether the history meets the NSW mandatory reporting threshold.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, RR 22 /min, T 37.0 deg C
data_table.Examination: Well child, no bruising, no injury, growth 25th centile
data_table.Social context: Informal care with grandmother, mother in relapse, AOD engaged, no current direct witness of violence
data_table.History: Fully immunised, attending school, no developmental regression

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step regarding mandatory reporting?

options.text: Make an immediate report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline today because the mother is in current relapse with active methamphetamine use disorder
options.rationale: Parental substance use alone does not meet the NSW RoSH threshold; the question is whether current circumstances place the child at risk. Would be correct if there were evidence of active child neglect or harm now (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).

options.text: Refer the mother to a parenting program and document the family concerns clearly without taking any further statutory action today
options.rationale: Documenting without using the Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool risks missing the RoSH threshold and is not consistent with reporter duties. Would be appropriate only after MRG indicates below-threshold (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).

options.text: Take no further action today because the child is now in informal kinship care with the maternal grandmother and is well
options.rationale: Informal kinship care does not extinguish the reporter's duty; the MRG must still be applied. Would be correct only after MRG confirms below-threshold (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).

options.text: Notify NSW Police directly today because of the disclosed domestic violence between the mother and the stepfather in the past month
options.rationale: Police are notified for criminal matters but child welfare goes via the Child Protection Helpline after using the MRG. Would be correct in parallel for active criminal violence, not as the sole pathway for welfare concerns (Source: NSW Health Child Wellbeing Unit).

options.text: Use the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool to determine whether the Risk of Significant Harm threshold is met before reporting or referring
options.rationale: Mandatory reporters in NSW use the Mandatory Reporter Guide structured decision tool to determine whether the Risk of Significant Harm threshold is met; sub-ROSH concerns are referred to Family Referral Services or the Child Wellbeing Unit, as per the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

explanation.summary: In NSW, mandatory reporters use the Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool to determine whether concerns meet the Risk of Significant Harm threshold. Sub-ROSH concerns are managed through Family Referral Services and the local Child Wellbeing Unit; ROSH-level concerns are reported to the Child Protection Helpline.
key_points: Threshold = Risk of Significant Harm (ROSH), not 'concern'
Use the structured Mandatory Reporter Guide tool, not gut feeling
Sub-ROSH = Family Referral Services or Child Wellbeing Unit
ROSH = Child Protection Helpline 132 111
Parental substance use alone is not automatically ROSH
explanation.pearls: Document the Mandatory Reporter Guide outcome itself; this is the medico-legal record.

stem: A paediatric registrar at a metropolitan Victorian public hospital is asked to review an 11-year-old boy admitted after an episode of self-harm at boarding school. During the admission the boy discloses that a residential care worker employed by the boarding school, who is not a teacher, has been entering his bedroom at night for the past three weeks and tickling and wrestling with him in a way that has made him uncomfortable. There has been no disclosed penetrative contact and no injury, but the boy describes the contact as making him anxious and not wanting to return to the school. The boy's parents live overseas and the school is the legal guardian during term. The registrar discusses the case with the consultant paediatrician on call. The hospital has both Child Protection and Police pathways available, and the consultant raises the additional question of whether the conduct falls under the Victorian Reportable Conduct Scheme administered by the Commission for Children and Young People.

data_table.Examination: Well, no acute injury, multiple superficial linear forearm abrasions, age-appropriate Tanner stage
data_table.Mental state: Anxious, congruent affect, no current SI, no psychotic symptoms
data_table.Social context: Boarding school in loco parentis, parents overseas, identified worker is residential care staff
data_table.History: First admission, no prior DHHS / Department of Families involvement, fully immunised

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate combination of statutory pathways for this disclosure?

options.text: Report to Victoria Police and Child Protection only and skip the Reportable Conduct Scheme today because it only covers schoolteachers and not residential care workers
options.rationale: The Reportable Conduct Scheme covers schools, residential care and a wide range of organisations, not only classroom teachers. Would be correct only if the alleged perpetrator were outside an in-scope organisation (Source: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria).
source_refs: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme

options.text: Make a Child Protection report alone because the boy is currently admitted to hospital and is safe under medical supervision
options.rationale: Hospital admission does not remove the duty to notify Police of possible criminal conduct or to trigger the Reportable Conduct Scheme. Would never be correct (Source: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria).
source_refs: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme

options.text: Notify the Department of Families and Victoria Police of possible offending, and have the school notify the Commission under the Reportable Conduct Scheme
options.rationale: Three statutory pathways apply in parallel: Child Protection notification for the child's safety, Police for possible criminal conduct, and the Reportable Conduct Scheme via the head of the entity to the Commission for Children and Young People, as per Better Health Channel and the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting
Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme
Victoria Police Sexual Offences and Child Abuse Investigation

options.text: Notify the school principal alone and ask the principal to manage the staff member and any subsequent reports internally
options.rationale: Internal management does not satisfy mandatory reporting or the parallel Reportable Conduct Scheme obligations; both sit outside the school's HR process. Would never be correct (Source: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting).
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

options.text: Defer all statutory reporting until the boy has completed a forensic interview with the Victorian Forensic Paediatric Medical Service later this week
options.rationale: Reports are made on reasonable belief; forensic interview is arranged in parallel, not as a prerequisite. Delaying notification breaches the statutory duty. Would never be correct (Source: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting).
source_refs: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting

explanation.summary: In Victoria, three pathways may run in parallel for institutional child abuse concerns: mandatory Child Protection notification, Police notification of suspected criminal conduct, and the Reportable Conduct Scheme administered by the Commission for Children and Young People when the alleged perpetrator works in a covered organisation.
key_points: Reportable Conduct Scheme covers schools, residential care, OOHC, religious bodies and more
Head of the entity must notify the Commission within 3 business days
Mandatory CP report is to the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing
Police notification covers suspected criminal offending
Pathways run in parallel, not sequentially
explanation.pearls: If the alleged perpetrator works for a covered organisation, the Reportable Conduct Scheme runs alongside the standard Child Protection pathway.

sources.label: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria Reportable Conduct Scheme
Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting
Victoria Police Sexual Offences and Child Abuse Investigation

stem: A 9-month-old boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by his father for assessment of a single bruise noted that morning while changing the nappy. The bruise is on the left buttock, measures 2 cm by 3 cm, and is yellow-green in colour. The father is unsure how the bruise occurred but suggests the baby may have rolled against a cot rail. The infant is not yet pulling to stand and only cruises with support. He was born at term with a normal newborn check, is fully immunised, growing along the 50th centile, and has met all developmental milestones to date. He is otherwise well, feeding normally, and there is no history of bleeding disorder, no family history of coagulopathy, and no other current bruising visible. Examination is otherwise unremarkable with no torn frenulum, no posterior rib tenderness, and no facial findings. The triage nurse asks the ED registrar to apply the TEN-4-FACESp clinical decision rule to determine whether further child protection assessment is warranted. The father is co-operative and the mother is at home with siblings.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124 bpm, RR 28 /min, T 36.9 deg C, weight 50th centile
data_table.Examination: Single 2 x 3 cm yellow-green bruise left buttock, no other bruising, no torn frenulum, no rib tenderness
data_table.Development: Cruises with support, not yet pulling to stand, no rolling onto furniture
data_table.History: Fully immunised, no medications, no FH bleeding disorder

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge the infant home today with parental reassurance because a single small bruise is within normal infant variation
options.rationale: Any bruise in a non-cruising infant is TEN-4-FACESp positive and a sentinel injury; 'normal variant' is the dismissal anti-pattern. Would be acceptable only in a mobile cruiser/walker with bruising in expected sites (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

options.text: Refer for routine outpatient haematology review to exclude an inherited bleeding disorder before any safeguarding concerns are raised
options.rationale: Bleeding-disorder screen is appropriate but does not replace urgent safeguarding for a sentinel injury; running coags alone is the medicalisation-of-abuse anti-pattern. Would be sufficient only if safeguarding has already cleared and there is family bleeding history (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

options.text: Order a plain X-ray of the affected limb today and review the infant in 48 hours if any new symptoms develop
options.rationale: Single-region X-ray misses clinically silent rib, metaphyseal and skull fractures; skeletal survey is the standard imaging for suspected NAI under 2 years. Would be appropriate only in older children with localised injury and no safeguarding concern (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse; RANZCR).

options.text: Admit for formal non-accidental injury workup with senior paediatric review, social work, skeletal survey, and mandatory child protection notification
options.rationale: The TEN-4-FACESp rule flags any bruising in non-mobile infants and bruising on the torso, ear, neck, frenulum, angle of jaw, cheeks, eyelids and subconjunctivae as concerning for abuse; formal NAI workup and mandatory notification are required, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and the Pierce TEN-4-FACESp rule.

options.text: Photograph and document the bruise today, then arrange a routine general practitioner review for the following week
options.rationale: Photo-and-defer misses time-critical assessment for sentinel injury in a non-mobile infant. Documentation supplements but does not replace acute admission and notification (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

explanation.summary: TEN-4-FACESp identifies high-specificity bruising patterns for abuse: bruising on the Torso, Ear, Neck in children 4 years or younger; any bruising in infants under 4 months; and bruising on the Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheeks, Eyelids, Subconjunctivae, or patterned bruising at any age. Any positive feature mandates formal child protection assessment.
key_points: Non-mobile infants do not get bruises by accident
Sentinel injuries precede severe abuse in 25-30% of fatal cases
Workup: skeletal survey, retinal exam, head imaging if <12 months
Mandatory notification is parallel to medical workup
Bleeding disorder screen included, does not delay safeguarding
explanation.pearls: 'Those who don't cruise, rarely bruise' is the catchphrase that flags this every time.

stem: A 5-week-old girl is referred to a tertiary paediatric ED by her general practitioner with a 1 cm torn upper labial frenulum that the mother attributes to a recent bottle feed. The baby was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery, vitamin K was given, and she has been feeding adequately with appropriate weight gain. She has no other visible injuries and no current bleeding. The parents live together, both are employed, and there has been no previous contact with Child Protection. The infant is fully immunised to age and there is no family history of bleeding disorder, osteogenesis imperfecta, or sudden infant death. On examination there is the torn frenulum, an otherwise normal mouth, no posterior rib tenderness, normal anterior fontanelle, no head circumference change from birth percentile, and a normal neurological exam. Bedside coagulation screen and FBC are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142 bpm, RR 42 /min, T 36.8 deg C, weight tracking 50th centile
data_table.Examination: Torn upper labial frenulum 1 cm, no other injury, normal fontanelle, normal eyes externally, no rib tenderness
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, INR 1.1, APTT normal, fibrinogen normal
data_table.History: Fully immunised, vitamin K given at birth, no FH bleeding

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate complete sentinel injury workup for this infant?

options.text: Skeletal survey now and at 11 to 14 days, neuroimaging, dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy, coagulation and bone studies, and forensic paediatric involvement
options.rationale: Standard NAI workup in an infant under 12 months with a sentinel injury includes initial and follow-up skeletal survey, neuroimaging, dilated indirect retinal exam by ophthalmology, bleeding and bone disorder screen, and forensic paediatric assessment, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and RANZCR.
source_refs: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

options.text: Skeletal survey performed once at admission only today, with no follow-up skeletal imaging and no dilated formal eye examination
options.rationale: Repeat skeletal survey at 11-14 days substantially improves detection of healing rib and metaphyseal fractures; single survey alone is incomplete. Would be acceptable only in older mobile children with localised injury (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse; RANZCR).
source_refs: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

options.text: MRI brain only today, with no skeletal imaging and no formal eye examination given the absence of bruising elsewhere on the body
options.rationale: MRI brain alone misses occult fractures; skeletal survey is mandatory even without external fracture evidence because clinically silent fractures are common. Would never alone be sufficient (Source: RANZCR).
source_refs: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

options.text: Bedside eye examination by the paediatric registrar without pupil dilation today, with no skeletal imaging and no brain imaging arranged
options.rationale: Retinal haemorrhage detection requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by an ophthalmologist; bedside undilated fundoscopy misses retinal haemorrhages. Would never be sufficient (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

options.text: Coagulation screen alone and a routine outpatient paediatric follow-up arranged because the injury appears small and isolated today
options.rationale: Sentinel injuries in non-mobile infants predict escalating abuse; outpatient follow-up alone is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

explanation.summary: Sentinel injuries (torn frenulum, bruise in non-mobile infant, intra-oral injury) trigger formal NAI workup: skeletal survey now and at 11-14 days, neuroimaging (CT or MRI), dilated indirect retinal exam by ophthalmology, coagulation and bone studies, plus parallel Child Protection and forensic paediatric service involvement.
key_points: Sentinel injuries precede serious abuse in 25-30% of fatal cases
Two skeletal surveys (now and 11-14 days) increases yield
Retinal exam requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by ophthalmologist
Coagulation and bone screen rules out mimics, does not replace workup
Forensic paediatric service (VFPMS in Vic, NSW CASS equivalents) involved early
explanation.pearls: A torn frenulum in a non-mobile infant is the sentinel injury most commonly missed in primary care.

sources.label: RANZCR Skeletal Survey for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury

stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric developmental clinic for behavioural and learning difficulties. He was placed in long-term foster care at 3 months. His birth mother is documented to have consumed alcohol at hazardous levels throughout pregnancy. School reports document impulsivity, poor adaptive functioning, and specific maths and language deficits despite intensive support. Cognitive testing (educational psychologist) shows FSIQ 78 with significant scatter and severe impairment in three neurodevelopmental domains (executive function, adaptive behaviour, language). Physical growth tracks the 25th centile. On focused facial examination: smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, and short palpebral fissures (below 3rd centile for age) - all three sentinel features present. There is no head injury, no seizure disorder and no other syndromic features. The foster carer is engaged with an Aboriginal community-controlled health service.

data_table.Growth: Height and weight 25th centile, OFC 25th centile
data_table.Facial features: Smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures (<3rd centile)
data_table.Cognitive: FSIQ 78, severe impairment in executive function, adaptive behaviour, and language domains
data_table.Prenatal: Confirmed hazardous prenatal alcohol exposure throughout pregnancy
data_table.History: Foster care from 3 months, fully immunised, no other syndromic features

lead_in: Which Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD category best applies?

options.text: Foetal Alcohol Syndrome without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure
options.rationale: Exposure is confirmed; this category does not apply. Would be correct only when prenatal exposure history cannot be confirmed but features are otherwise consistent (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD).
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: ADHD combined presentation alone
options.rationale: ADHD may coexist but does not capture facial dysmorphology or the breadth of FASD-specific neurodevelopmental impairment. Would be correct as a stand-alone diagnosis only without FASD features (Source: DSM-5; Australian Guide to FASD).
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: FASD with less than three sentinel facial features
options.rationale: He has all three sentinel features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion, short palpebral fissures), so the less-than-three category does not apply (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD).
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Static encephalopathy of unknown cause because measured IQ is above 70
options.rationale: FSIQ over 70 does not exclude FASD; diagnosis is made on neurodevelopmental domain impairment, not global IQ. Would be correct only for a non-FASD aetiology (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD).
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: FASD with three sentinel facial features: all three sentinel features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures under 3rd centile), severe impairment in at least three of ten neurodevelopmental domains, and confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure
options.rationale: The Australian Guide diagnoses FASD with three sentinel facial features when all three are present together with severe impairment in three or more of ten neurodevelopmental domains and confirmed or assumed prenatal alcohol exposure - the specific AU diagnostic threshold tested here.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

explanation.summary: The Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD recognises two diagnostic categories: FASD with 3 sentinel facial features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures), and FASD with less than 3. Both require severe impairment in at least 3 of 10 neurodevelopmental domains and confirmed (or assumed) prenatal alcohol exposure.
key_points: Three sentinel facial features: smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, short palpebral fissures
Severe impairment in >=3 of 10 neurodevelopmental domains required
Prenatal alcohol exposure confirmed OR assumed
IQ alone does not make or exclude the diagnosis
Domains include cognition, executive function, language, adaptive, motor, memory, attention, affect, social, sensory
explanation.pearls: Sentinel facial features only develop with first trimester exposure during a narrow window; their absence does not exclude FASD.

sources.label: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to a tertiary ED by her mother for review after losing 12 kg over five months. She has been progressively restricting her intake, exercising compulsively, and has missed three menstrual periods. She is medically naive and on no medications. On arrival she is alert and oriented, denies suicidal ideation, but is cold to touch and describes near-syncope on standing this morning. Her current weight is 38 kg and her premorbid weight was 50 kg. Body mass index is 14.8 kg/m2 (well below the 3rd centile for age). Her supine heart rate is 38 beats per minute with a postural drop to systolic blood pressure of 78 mmHg standing. Core temperature is 35.4 deg C. Bedside ECG shows sinus bradycardia with a QTc of 470 ms. Bloods reveal potassium 3.0 mmol/L, phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, magnesium 0.65 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, and liver enzymes mildly raised with ALT 78 U/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 38 bpm supine, BP 96/60 supine to 78/52 standing, T 35.4 deg C, RR 14 /min
data_table.Anthropometrics: Weight 38 kg, BMI 14.8 kg/m2 (<3rd centile), 24% weight loss over 5 months
data_table.ECG: Sinus bradycardia, QTc 470 ms, no arrhythmia
data_table.Investigations: K 3.0 mmol/L, PO4 0.7 mmol/L, Mg 0.65 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, ALT 78 U/L
data_table.Mental state: Cognitively intact, no SI, mildly depressed mood, motivated to recover

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home today with twice-weekly community eating disorder team review and oral potassium and phosphate replacement at home
options.rationale: Multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria (HR under 40, postural BP drop, hypothermia, hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, QTc over 450 ms) mandate inpatient medical admission; outpatient is the under-triage anti-pattern. Would be appropriate only with low-risk physical parameters (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU Guide).

options.text: Admit to the paediatric ward under medical care with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding starting at 20 kcal/kg/day, electrolyte replacement, and ED team involvement
options.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU high risk criteria mandate medical admission with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding to prevent refeeding syndrome, electrolyte and glucose management, and dual physical and eating disorder team care, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Admit directly to a child and adolescent mental health inpatient unit today with full caloric refeeding at 50 kcal/kg/day starting from day one
options.rationale: Medical stabilisation precedes mental health admission in MARSIPAN-AU high-risk; aggressive 50 kcal/kg/day refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome. Would be correct only after medical stabilisation and on a graded refeeding plan (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Transfer her today to a peripheral hospital for rapid IV crystalloid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg boluses and overnight monitoring
options.rationale: Aggressive crystalloid bolus risks cardiac failure in the malnourished heart; cautious volume correction with electrolyte replacement is needed, not resuscitation-style fluids. Would be correct only in haemorrhagic or septic shock unrelated to malnutrition (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Refer her today for outpatient review in an eating disorder day program with weekly bloods, ECG monitoring and a six week follow up plan
options.rationale: Day program does not address immediate medical risk; high-risk physical criteria mandate inpatient admission. Would be correct only for medically stable patients with adequate community supports (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).

explanation.summary: MARSIPAN-AU defines medical admission criteria for severe restrictive eating disorders. High risk features (bradycardia <40, postural BP drop >20 mmHg systolic, hypothermia <35.5, K <3.0, PO4 <0.5, glucose <3.0, QTc >450 ms, rapid weight loss >1 kg/week, BMI <14 in adolescents) mandate inpatient care with cardiac monitoring and cautious refeeding.
key_points: Bradycardia <40 bpm = high risk
Postural drop >20 mmHg systolic = high risk
Hypothermia <35.5 deg C = high risk
Refeeding starts low (20 kcal/kg/day) and titrates
Dual medical + eating disorder team is the standard
explanation.pearls: The malnourished heart can fail on aggressive crystalloid bolus; treat the metabolic risk first.

stem: A 17-year-old girl presents to her general practitioner for dental review following her dentist's discovery of bilateral enamel erosion on the lingual surfaces of her upper incisors. On gentle questioning she discloses six months of binge eating once or twice weekly followed by self-induced vomiting, with intermittent use of laxatives. She maintains a body mass index of 22 kg/m2 with stable weight. She describes a marked sense of loss of control during binges, intense subsequent shame, and persistent overvaluation of body shape and weight. Her menstrual cycle remains regular and she is not currently using any contraception. She denies suicidal ideation but has felt low in mood and isolated from friends. Examination is unremarkable apart from mild parotid swelling and Russell's sign on the dorsum of one hand. Bedside ECG shows sinus rhythm with QTc of 410 ms. Bloods show potassium 3.4 mmol/L, bicarbonate 30 mmol/L, with normal phosphate, magnesium, and glucose. She lives at home with both parents and is in Year 12 studying for the HSC. She has not previously sought help.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, BMI 22 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Bilateral parotid swelling, Russell's sign, dental erosion, no postural hypotension
data_table.Investigations: K 3.4 mmol/L, HCO3 30 mmol/L, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, ECG QTc 410 ms
data_table.Mental state: Low mood, shame, no suicidal ideation, body image overvaluation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer her today for inpatient medical admission to a paediatric ward for refeeding under the MARSIPAN-AU protocol
options.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU medical admission criteria are not met (BMI normal, electrolytes near range, no bradycardia, normal QTc); inpatient admission is reserved for high physical risk. Would be correct only with high-risk vitals or electrolytes (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards Eating Disorders

options.text: Reassure her today and advise dietary balance with no specialist referral because weight is normal and physical risk is low
options.rationale: Bulimia carries significant medical and psychological morbidity even at normal weight; reassurance-only misses the disorder. Would be correct only in occasional subthreshold disordered eating without functional impairment (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards).
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards Eating Disorders

options.text: Prescribe high dose fluoxetine 60 mg daily today as first line monotherapy without any structured psychological therapy
options.rationale: Fluoxetine 60 mg may add benefit for bulimia in adults but CBT-ED is first-line; pharmacotherapy alone is not standard. Would be appropriate after CBT-ED failure or as add-on (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorder Guidelines).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines Eating Disorders

options.text: Commence outpatient enhanced cognitive behaviour therapy for eating disorders with GP medical monitoring and dental follow up
options.rationale: First line treatment for bulimia nervosa in adolescents is enhanced CBT-ED in the outpatient setting with regular medical monitoring of electrolytes, weight, and ECG, as per ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards and RANZCP Eating Disorder Guidelines.
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards Eating Disorders
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines Eating Disorders

options.text: Prescribe oral potassium chloride supplementation today and review her in three months with no psychological intervention
options.rationale: Electrolyte replacement is supportive but does not treat the disorder; CBT-ED is the definitive first-line. Would be appropriate as a supportive adjunct alongside CBT-ED, not in isolation (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards).
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards Eating Disorders

explanation.summary: Bulimia nervosa in adolescents is treated first line with enhanced cognitive behaviour therapy for eating disorders (CBT-ED) on an outpatient basis, supported by general practitioner medical monitoring and dental care. Fluoxetine 60 mg may be added but is not first line monotherapy.
key_points: Russell's sign, parotid swelling, dental erosion: classic exam signs
Hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from vomiting
First line: enhanced CBT-ED outpatient
Fluoxetine 60 mg is the SSRI of choice if pharmacotherapy added
Inpatient admission only if MARSIPAN-AU physical risk criteria met
explanation.pearls: Bulimic patients often present at normal weight; do not let normal BMI delay specialist referral.

sources.label: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards Eating Disorders
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines Eating Disorders

stem: A 16-year-old boy is seen in general practice with a 6-month history of being unable to fall asleep until 0200 to 0300 despite being in bed from 2200, severely impaired wake on school mornings at 0630, and missing first period most days. On weekends and during recent school holidays he sleeps from approximately 0300 to 1200 and wakes refreshed and functioning well. He uses his phone in bed, denies caffeine after midday, denies illicit substance use, denies snoring or witnessed apnoeas, and a HEEADSSS screen is otherwise reassuring with no self-harm or pervasive low mood. His mother brought him today after two unsuccessful attempts at simply enforcing an earlier bedtime over the past month. He is otherwise well, on no medications, and his examination is unremarkable. A sleep diary over the past 14 days confirms a stable late sleep onset with normal total sleep duration when free running. You diagnose delayed sleep-wake phase disorder and discuss management with him and his mother.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 64 bpm, BP 112/68 mmHg, BMI 22 kg/m2
data_table.Sleep diary: School nights: sleep onset 0200-0300, wake 0630; weekends: sleep onset 0300, wake 1200
data_table.Examination: Mallampati 1, no tonsillar hypertrophy, no obesity, alert in clinic
data_table.History: No medications, no substance use, occasional phone use in bed, no snoring

lead_in: Which initial management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Sleep hygiene counselling alone, with planned review in 6 weeks before considering any pharmacological or light-based measure
options.rationale: Sleep hygiene alone is insufficient once delayed sleep-wake phase disorder is established with impaired daytime function; behavioural plus chronotherapeutic measures are required from the outset, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia and RCH CPG: Sleep problems.

options.text: Low-dose melatonin 0.5 mg taken 5 to 7 hours before current sleep onset, with morning bright light and gradual schedule advance
options.rationale: Phase-advancing low-dose melatonin (0.3 to 0.5 mg) taken several hours before current sleep onset, combined with morning bright light and gradual schedule advance, is the evidence-based first-line regimen in Australian adolescent practice. The timing (well before bedtime, not at bedtime) and low dose are the levers that distinguish this from sleep-onset insomnia treatment, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia and RCH CPG: Sleep problems.

options.text: Melatonin 3 mg at the desired bedtime of 2200 with screen reduction advice and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Higher-dose melatonin at desired bedtime acts as a hypnotic rather than a phase-shifter; the chronobiotic effect requires a low dose taken hours before current sleep onset, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia.

options.text: Trial of temazepam at 2200 for 2 weeks to entrain an earlier sleep onset, then taper while consolidating sleep hygiene
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not indicated for circadian phase disorders in adolescents and risk dependence and rebound; they do not shift the circadian phase, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia.

options.text: Referral for cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as the sole initial intervention before any light or melatonin trial
options.rationale: CBT-I targets sleep-onset and maintenance insomnia, not a phase-delayed circadian rhythm; the primary lever is phase shifting (bright light AM, low-dose melatonin PM) with behavioural support, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia.

explanation.summary: Delayed sleep phase syndrome (DSPS) is the most common circadian rhythm disorder in adolescents. Sleep onset is consistently delayed; total sleep duration is normal when allowed to free run. Management combines sleep hygiene, scheduled morning bright light, evening blue light avoidance, and timed low dose melatonin.
key_points: Prevalence peaks at 7-16% in adolescents
Phase delay, not insomnia
Free-running weekend pattern is the diagnostic clue
Treatment: bright light AM, melatonin PM, screen reduction
Polysomnography normal architecture
explanation.pearls: If they sleep well from 3 am to midday on weekends, it is a clock problem, not an insomnia problem.

sources.label: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to a general practice by his parents with a six month history of nightly loud snoring with witnessed pauses in breathing followed by gasping arousals. He sleeps in unusual positions with neck extended and his parents describe loud restless sleep, occasional bed wetting, and morning headaches. His behaviour at preschool has changed with increased hyperactivity, poor concentration, and emerging difficulty with letter recognition. He is not overweight, with growth tracking the 50th centile, and is fully immunised. He has had three episodes of acute otitis media in the past year and a current persistent mouth breathing pattern. On examination he has Brodsky grade 3 tonsillar hypertrophy that meets in the midline, an enlarged adenoid pad inferred from chronic mouth breathing, no Mallampati view assessable due to tonsillar size, and an otherwise normal cardiovascular examination with no parasternal heave. There is no craniofacial syndrome, no neuromuscular disorder, and no family history of sudden cardiac death. He has no Indigenous status documented and lives in metropolitan Sydney with both parents and a younger sister.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 22 /min, BMI 16.0 kg/m2 (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Brodsky grade 3 tonsils, chronic mouth breathing, no parasternal heave, no hepatomegaly
data_table.Behaviour: Hyperactivity, poor concentration, declining letter recognition
data_table.History: Witnessed apnoeas, gasping arousals, morning headaches, secondary enuresis

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of intranasal corticosteroid spray for 12 weeks with a parental sleep diary and clinical review at end of trial
options.rationale: Intranasal steroids help mild OSA with adenoidal hypertrophy, but witnessed apnoeas, hyperactivity and tonsillar hypertrophy require ENT and sleep referral first. Would be correct in mild snoring without apnoea or daytime impairment (Source: RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea).

options.text: Commence stimulant methylphenidate based on the school report of hyperactivity and inattention noted in class
options.rationale: Untreated OSA fully mimics ADHD; starting stimulants before addressing sleep is the classic misdiagnosis anti-pattern. Would be correct only after OSA is treated and ADHD persists (Source: RACP Australian Guideline on ADHD).

options.text: Trial of CPAP at home overnight pending review without prior ENT review or sleep service involvement arranged
options.rationale: CPAP is reserved for residual OSA after adenotonsillectomy or where surgery is contraindicated; not first-line in a previously untreated child with surgical anatomy. Would be correct as second-line after failed surgery (Source: RCH CPG: OSA).

options.text: Reassure the family that loud snoring is developmentally normal in a 5-year-old and arrange routine review in 12 months
options.rationale: Snoring with witnessed apnoeas, behavioural change and morning headaches is clinical OSA, not a normal variant. Would never be correct in this presentation (Source: RCH CPG: OSA).

options.text: Refer to ENT for adenotonsillectomy assessment with paediatric sleep service involvement and consideration of polysomnography
options.rationale: Adenotonsillectomy is the first line treatment for paediatric OSA with adenotonsillar hypertrophy; ENT referral with sleep service involvement and PSG if available is the recommended pathway, as per RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea and ATS Statement on Paediatric OSA.
source_refs: ATS Clinical Practice Guideline Paediatric OSA

explanation.summary: Paediatric OSA red flags include witnessed apnoeas, gasping arousals, restless sleep with neck extension, mouth breathing, morning headaches, secondary enuresis, and behavioural change. First line treatment in children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy is adenotonsillectomy with ENT and sleep service involvement.
key_points: Witnessed apnoeas + tonsillar hypertrophy = surgical pathway
OSA mimics ADHD; treat sleep before stimulants
Polysomnography is gold standard but ENT referral is not delayed waiting
CPAP reserved for residual OSA after surgery
Untreated OSA risks cor pulmonale and growth failure
explanation.pearls: Always ask about sleep BEFORE treating apparent ADHD in a snoring preschooler.

sources.label: ATS Clinical Practice Guideline Paediatric OSA

stem: An 11-year-old girl is referred by her general practitioner to a community paediatric service for assessment of probable attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. School and parent Conners 3 rating scales show clinically elevated inattention and hyperactive impulsive symptoms present since early primary school across home and school. Her mother reports persistent difficulty falling asleep, low mood since the start of the year with tearfulness on most school days, episodes of refusing to attend school, and a recent disclosure of being bullied for two years. She avoids social activities, has lost two close friendships, and her academic performance has dropped sharply this year. She is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile, takes no regular medications and has no medical comorbidity. Her hearing and vision screens at school have been normal in the past 12 months. There is no history of trauma, no head injury, no substance use, and no Child Protection involvement. The paediatrician is preparing to confirm a diagnosis using DSM-5 criteria and is considering whether and how to assess for comorbid conditions before any pharmacological treatment is considered.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 108/64 mmHg, growth 50th centile
data_table.Rating scales: Conners 3 inattention T-score 78, hyperactive-impulsive T-score 72 (both parent and teacher)
data_table.Social context: Two year bullying history, two friendship losses, school refusal episodes
data_table.Mental state: Tearful, low mood, sleep onset difficulty, no SI elicited but not formally screened

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step before starting medication?

options.text: Start immediate release methylphenidate today at low dose and titrate the dose while planning ongoing structured review
options.rationale: AADPA guidance requires systematic comorbidity assessment (anxiety, depression, learning disorder, sleep, substance use, trauma) before pharmacotherapy because comorbidities alter the plan. Would be appropriate after structured screen confirms uncomplicated ADHD (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).

options.text: Refer her today for full neuropsychological testing battery before any other clinical intervention or medication is considered
options.rationale: Routine neuropsychological battery is not required to confirm ADHD or guide pharmacotherapy. Would be appropriate only for diagnostic uncertainty or suspected specific learning disorder (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).

options.text: Perform structured assessment for common ADHD comorbidities with validated screens and address them in parallel with the ADHD treatment plan
options.rationale: The Australian guideline mandates structured assessment for common comorbidities at diagnosis because comorbidity rates exceed 60% and shape both the order and choice of intervention, as per the AADPA ADHD Guideline.

options.text: Refer her back to the school to manage bullying alone and review again in 6 months before any further clinical intervention
options.rationale: School bullying management is part of the plan but does not justify deferring diagnostic completion. Would never be correct as the sole next step (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).

options.text: Prescribe melatonin alone tonight for sleep without further ADHD-specific intervention or any structured comorbidity assessment
options.rationale: Sleep is one of multiple domains; isolated melatonin without comprehensive comorbidity assessment misses the broader picture. Would be correct only as adjunct after full screen (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).

explanation.summary: The Australian Evidence Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD requires structured assessment for common comorbidities (anxiety, depression, learning disorder, ASD, sleep, tic, substance use, trauma) at diagnosis. Comorbidities are present in 60% or more and shape the order and choice of treatment.
key_points: Comorbidity rate >60% in paediatric ADHD
Screen at diagnosis, not after starting stimulants
Sleep disorders may mimic or worsen ADHD
Depression / anxiety often need treatment first
Bullying and trauma history are essential history elements
explanation.pearls: Untreated comorbid depression often blunts apparent stimulant response.

stem: A 13-year-old girl in Year 7 has missed 18 of the last 30 school days. She presents on school mornings with abdominal pain, nausea, and tearfulness and is unable to leave the house once school is mentioned. She reports being terrified of going to school but cannot name a specific bully, teacher, or academic concern. She is happy to spend time with friends on weekends, attends netball training without difficulty, and goes to her grandmother's house overnight without distress. She sleeps in her own room without separation distress. At a recent paediatric review the school counsellor noted that her academic performance was previously above average and a transition from primary to secondary school in February has been associated with the new symptoms. There is no history of trauma, no bullying disclosed, no substance use, no self-harm, no Child Protection involvement. She is fully immunised, growing along the 50th centile, takes no medications, and has no medical disorder identified. On HEEADSSS the only significant finding is the school distress. Her parents are supportive and engaged.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, BMI 21 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peritonism, well outside school context
data_table.Mental state: Anxious on school mornings, euthymic at weekends, no SI, no psychotic symptoms
data_table.History: Year 7 transition February, no bullying disclosed, peer relationships intact

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Separation anxiety disorder with persistent excessive distress on separation from her primary attachment figures during the day
options.rationale: She separates without distress at netball, weekends and overnight at grandmother's; distress is school-specific not separation-driven. Would be correct only with distress on all separations from primary attachment figures (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Conduct disorder with truancy and aggressive antisocial behaviour directed toward both peers and classroom teachers across multiple settings and contexts
options.rationale: She is distressed about not attending and not engaging in delinquent behaviour; this is the opposite of conduct disorder truancy. Would be correct only with rule violation and aggression (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: School refusal driven by anxiety in the context of a recent school transition with no co-occurring conduct disorder features
options.rationale: School refusal is the picture - clinically anxious, somatic on school mornings, intact functioning outside school, transition trigger - and is the recommended formulation for anxiety driven school avoidance without truancy or conduct features, as per Raising Children Network and RANZCP.

options.text: Functional abdominal pain alone with no underlying comorbid mental health driver and full resolution outside the school environment
options.rationale: Pain occurs only on school mornings, with anticipatory distress and avoidance, and resolves at weekends; somatic symptoms are part of the anxiety pattern, not isolated functional pain. Would be correct only if pain were context-independent (Source: Raising Children Network School Refusal).

options.text: Major depressive disorder with pervasive social withdrawal and anhedonia present across both school and non-school contexts
options.rationale: She engages happily on weekends and shows no anhedonia outside school; not depressive. Would be correct only with pervasive low mood and anhedonia across contexts (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders Guidelines).
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

explanation.summary: School refusal is anxiety driven school avoidance distinct from truancy: the child wants to be at school but is too distressed to attend. Treatment is a graded return to school with cognitive behaviour therapy, family support, and school engagement.
key_points: School-specific distress, NOT generalised separation difficulty
Distinguish from truancy (no academic concern, family knows)
Somatic symptoms (gut, headache) on school mornings only
Treatment: graded return + CBT + school collaboration
Transition periods (Year 7, after holidays) are common triggers
explanation.pearls: Watch for the weekend-functioning pattern; it is diagnostic for school refusal over separation anxiety.

sources.label: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

stem: A 13-year-old boy is seen at a community child and adolescent mental health service after 18 months of escalating behaviour: repeated school fights, theft from a local shop, deliberate fire-setting in a school rubbish bin, cruelty to the family cat on multiple occasions, breaking into a neighbour's shed and damaging tools, lying, breaking curfew, and overnight absconding. He shows little remorse. He has had episodes of defiance and rule-refusal since age 6, but the present behaviours represent a clear escalation. There is no documented substance use disorder, no psychotic features, no manic features, and no significant trauma history disclosed. He has no intellectual disability and attends mainstream Year 8 inconsistently. He is up to date with immunisations and his physical examination is normal. He meets DSM-5 criteria for adolescent-onset conduct disorder. His parents are exhausted, his school principal has indicated that one more incident will result in expulsion, and he and his parents have agreed to engage with treatment.

data_table.History: Fights, theft, fire-setting, animal cruelty, breaking and entering, lying, curfew breaking, low remorse
data_table.Duration: 18 months of escalating behaviour, defiant features from age 6
data_table.Mental state: No psychotic features, no manic features, no substance use disorder
data_table.Examination: Well, normal cardio-respiratory, no neurological deficit

lead_in: Which first-line evidence-based intervention is most appropriate to recommend?

options.text: Commence risperidone 0.5 mg twice daily to target aggression and impulsivity, with monitoring of weight and metabolic parameters
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for conduct disorder and are reserved for severe persistent aggression unresponsive to psychosocial intervention, given significant metabolic and movement adverse effects, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines and RCH CPG: Behaviour.

options.text: Refer for structured family-based behavioural intervention (parent management training or multisystemic therapy); screen and treat comorbid ADHD, mood, substance use
options.rationale: Australian (RANZCP) and international evidence support structured family- and parent-based behavioural interventions (parent management training, multisystemic therapy, functional family therapy) as first-line for conduct disorder, alongside active screening and treatment of comorbid ADHD, mood, substance use and trauma, which co-occur in the majority of cases and worsen prognosis if missed, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines and headspace Clinical Toolkit.

options.text: Recommend a boot-camp style residential discipline programme to interrupt the current escalating behavioural pattern in a structured environment
options.rationale: Boot-camp and Scared Straight programmes have shown null or harmful effects on conduct disorder outcomes in randomised studies and are explicitly not recommended, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines. This is the anti-pattern tested here.

options.text: Individual psychodynamic psychotherapy weekly for 12 months as the sole intervention without parent or systemic involvement
options.rationale: Individual psychotherapy without family and systemic components is less effective than parent- and family-based interventions for conduct disorder in adolescents, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines.

options.text: Mandatory notification to police and child protection only, deferring any mental-health intervention until the legal pathway concludes fully
options.rationale: Engagement with justice and protective services where indicated does not replace evidence-based mental health treatment; delaying intervention worsens prognosis and is not consistent with stepped-care pathways, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines.

explanation.summary: Conduct disorder is a repetitive and persistent pattern of behaviour violating the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms with three or more of fifteen criteria in the past 12 months across aggression, property destruction, deceit/theft, and serious rule violation. ODD lacks these severe features.
key_points: Conduct disorder = rights violation + 3 of 15 criteria in 12 months
ODD = argumentative / defiant / vindictive without rights violation
Antisocial personality disorder requires age 18+
Adolescent-onset conduct disorder has a better prognosis than childhood-onset
Screen for comorbid ADHD, substance use, mood, trauma
explanation.pearls: Fire-setting + animal cruelty + theft together is the conduct disorder triad.

stem: A 15-year-old girl is reviewed by a child and adolescent psychiatrist after 8 weeks of structured cognitive behaviour therapy delivered weekly by a psychologist for a moderate to severe major depressive episode without psychotic features. She continues to have pervasive anhedonia, weight loss, early morning awakening, hopelessness, and passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. There is no substance use, no mania history, and she lives with both parents who are engaged in her care. She has no medical comorbidity, no QT prolonging medications, no known family history of medication intolerance, and her ECG is unremarkable with QTc 410 ms. Her clinician is now considering adding antidepressant pharmacotherapy. The family is concerned about the Therapeutic Goods Administration boxed warning regarding antidepressants and suicidal thinking in young people and wants to understand both the choice of medication and the monitoring required if treatment is started. The patient is open to medication trial with appropriate safety planning.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, T 36.6 deg C
data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms, no arrhythmia
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH all normal; urine pregnancy negative
data_table.Mental state: Pervasive anhedonia, early morning awakening, hopelessness, passive SI without plan
data_table.Medications: Nil regular, no QT prolonging agents

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate pharmacological choice with associated monitoring plan?

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 10 mg daily with weekly clinical review in the first 4 weeks for emergent suicidality, alongside ongoing CBT
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is the first line antidepressant in paediatric MDD with the strongest evidence base; the TGA boxed warning mandates close clinical monitoring (weekly initially) for emergent suicidal thinking and behaviour, alongside continued psychological therapy, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines and beyondblue Clinical Practice Guidelines.
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines
beyondblue Clinical Practice Guidelines Depression Adolescents

options.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg extended release daily with monthly clinical review and no additional suicidal thinking monitoring planned
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is not first-line in adolescents (discontinuation symptoms, less favourable safety); fluoxetine is preferred. Weekly suicidality monitoring is required regardless of agent. Would be correct only after SSRI failure under specialist care (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence paroxetine 20 mg daily with monthly clinical review and no formal safety planning arranged for the next four weeks
options.rationale: Paroxetine is not recommended in adolescents due to short half-life, severe discontinuation and unfavourable risk-benefit. Would never be correct in this age group (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 25 mg at night and titrate to therapeutic dose, with regular ECG monitoring planned over the coming months
options.rationale: TCAs are not effective in paediatric depression and carry overdose risk in an at-risk adolescent. Would never be correct for adolescent MDD (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Stop the current psychological therapy today and commence sertraline 50 mg daily as monotherapy without further structured psychological therapy
options.rationale: Combination psychological plus pharmacological therapy is recommended for moderate-severe adolescent depression; stopping therapy is the wrong direction. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
source_refs: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines

explanation.summary: Fluoxetine is first line antidepressant pharmacotherapy in adolescent major depressive disorder, used in combination with continued psychological therapy. The TGA boxed warning requires close clinical monitoring (typically weekly) for emergent suicidal thinking and behaviour during the first 4 weeks of treatment.
key_points: Fluoxetine = first line SSRI in adolescent MDD
Sertraline and escitalopram are second line
Boxed warning: emergent suicidal ideation in age <25
Weekly review in first 4 weeks then fortnightly
Continue CBT alongside antidepressant
explanation.pearls: Paroxetine and venlafaxine are not first line in adolescents due to discontinuation and tolerability concerns.

sources.label: RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines
beyondblue Clinical Practice Guidelines Depression Adolescents

stem: A 14-year-old presents to a tertiary gender service for assessment having identified as a transgender boy for the past four years. He was assigned female at birth, has been consistently insistent and persistent regarding gender identity, has socially transitioned two years ago with family support including legal name change at school, and reports persistent and increasing distress related to ongoing pubertal development. Menarche occurred at age 12 and he reports menstrual periods as a major and worsening source of dysphoria over the past 18 months. He is at Tanner stage 4 breast and pubic hair development with regular menstrual cycles every 28 days. He has been supported by an experienced child and adolescent psychiatrist for the past 18 months with no current major mental health condition; previous mild depressive symptoms have improved substantially with social transition and ongoing family acceptance.

data_table.Pubertal staging: Tanner stage 4 breast and pubic hair, menarche at 12, regular cycles
data_table.Mental health: 18 months psychiatric care, prior mild depression improved with social transition
data_table.Bone health: Bone age normal, no prior fractures, vitamin D 78 nmol/L, DEXA scheduled
data_table.Investigations: LH and FSH baseline normal, oestradiol mid follicular, normal pelvic ultrasound
data_table.Family + MDT: Both parents supportive, MDT includes endocrinology, psychiatry, gynaecology, fertility counselling

lead_in: According to the RCH Australian Standards of Care, what is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Commence gender affirming testosterone immediately because the patient is past Tanner stage 2 and has already menstruated for some years
options.rationale: Gender-affirming hormones in under-16s are introduced through structured pathway with MDT consensus and informed consent including fertility; immediate testosterone without staged approach is not standard. Would be appropriate at a later stage after suppression and structured consent (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Commence GnRH analogue puberty suppression to halt further pubertal progression with informed consent, MDT review, and ongoing bone, mood, and fertility monitoring
options.rationale: GnRH analogue puberty suppression is the typical stage 1 intervention to halt further pubertal progression and dysphoria from menses in this Tanner 4 adolescent with consistent gender identity, MDT support, and family agreement; consent does not require court authorisation per Re Kelvin, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines.
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines
AusPATH Position Statement on Trans Youth

options.text: Refer to the Family Court for authorisation of stage 1 puberty suppression as the required pathway under current Australian law
options.rationale: Court authorisation is no longer required for stage 1 puberty suppression in Australia after Re Jamie (2013) and Re Kelvin (2017) where parents and clinicians agree. Would never be required in current Australian practice (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Decline any medical intervention today until the patient reaches the age of 18 and continue with psychological support alone in the interim
options.rationale: Australian Standards explicitly do not require deferral to 18; staged medical intervention may begin earlier with MDT. Would be correct only with no MDT consensus or active contraindication (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Perform irreversible chest reconstruction surgery now to relieve dysphoria before any further pubertal progression continues to develop
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is not stage 1 in Australian adolescent gender-affirming care; reversible medical intervention precedes adult surgery. Would never be correct as the next step (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
source_refs: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines

explanation.summary: Australian Standards of Care for trans and gender diverse children and adolescents (RCH Melbourne) describe a staged pathway: stage 1 GnRH analogue puberty suppression, stage 2 gender affirming hormones, stage 3 surgery in adulthood. Court authorisation is not required where parents, clinicians and the Gillick competent young person agree (Re Kelvin 2017).
key_points: Stage 1: GnRH analogue puberty suppression (reversible)
Stage 2: gender affirming hormones (partially reversible)
Stage 3: surgery, generally in adulthood
Re Kelvin 2017: no court authorisation needed where consensus exists
MDT includes endocrinology, psychiatry, fertility counselling
explanation.pearls: Fertility counselling before stage 2 hormones is mandatory under the Australian Standards of Care.

sources.label: RCH Australian Standards of Care and Treatment Guidelines
AusPATH Position Statement on Trans Youth
